name: French Guianese Cassava Bread

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour

steps: Mix the cassava flour with enough water to form a firm, workable dough.
Press thin into a hot, lightly oiled skillet.
Cook 3-4 minutes per side until golden and dry.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour

steps_detailed: Mix the cassava flour with the coconut milk and enough water to form a firm, cohesive dough.
Press into a thin, even round in a hot, lightly oiled skillet.
Cook 4-5 minutes per side until golden, dry, and lightly crisp at the edges.
Cassava bread, made from the region's staple root crop, is an everyday French Guianese breakfast, often eaten with butter or jam.

name: French Guianese Bacon and Eggs Creole Style
steps: Fry the bacon until crisp.
Scramble the eggs with the jalapeno in the bacon fat.
Serve together with cassava bread if you have it.
steps_detailed: Fry the bacon in a skillet over medium heat until crisp, 4-5 minutes, then set aside.
Saute the onion and jalapeno in the rendered fat until softened.
Scramble the eggs into the pan and top with cilantro, serving alongside the bacon and cassava bread - a simple, Creole-inflected breakfast.

name: French Guianese Fruit with Lime
steps: Arrange the pineapple and mango slices on a plate.
Finish with a squeeze of lime juice.
steps_detailed: Arrange the pineapple and mango slices on a plate.
Finish with a generous squeeze of lime juice and a scattering of shredded coconut - French Guiana's tropical fruit, some of the finest in the region, makes for a simple, refreshing breakfast.

name: French Guianese Colombo de Poulet
steps: Brown the chicken with the turmeric and cumin.
Add the potato and a splash of water, and simmer, covered, 25-30 minutes until tender.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken with the onion, garlic, turmeric, and cumin over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the potato and 3/4 cup water, and simmer, covered, 28-35 minutes until the chicken and potato are tender.
Finish with the lime juice and serve with rice - colombo, a curry-style dish brought by Indian indentured laborers, is now deeply woven into French Guianese and wider French Caribbean cooking.

name: French Guianese Riz Creole
steps: Saute the bell pepper and onion in the oil until softened.
Toss with the cooked rice.
Serve as a simple side.
steps_detailed: Saute the bell pepper, onion, and garlic in the oil over medium heat, 6-8 minutes, until softened and fragrant.
Toss thoroughly with the cooked rice and cilantro.
Riz creole, simply seasoned rice, is the everyday starch accompanying most French Guianese meals.

name: French Guianese Blaff de Poisson
steps: Marinate the fish with the lime juice and garlic for 15-20 minutes.
Poach in simmering water with the jalapeno for 8-10 minutes until just cooked through.
Serve in the broth with rice.
steps_detailed: Marinate the fish with the lime juice and garlic for at least 20-30 minutes.
Bring a pot of water to a simmer with the jalapeno, onion, and cilantro, and poach the fish gently for 8-12 minutes until just cooked through and flaky.
Serve the fish in the fragrant broth with rice - blaff, a lightly spiced poached fish dish, is a Creole classic shared across France's Caribbean and South American territories.

name: French Guianese Roti
steps: Make a simple dough from the flour and water, and cook into a soft flatbread on a hot griddle.
Simmer the chicken with the turmeric and potato until tender.
Wrap the filling in the flatbread and serve.
steps_detailed: Make a soft dough from the flour, a pinch of salt, and water. Roll thin and cook on a hot, lightly oiled griddle, 1-2 minutes per side, until softly speckled.
Brown the chicken with the garlic and turmeric, then add the potato and a splash of water, and simmer, covered, 25-30 minutes until tender.
Wrap the filling generously in the warm flatbread - roti, brought by Indian indentured laborers, is now a beloved everyday French Guianese meal.

name: French Guianese Bouillon d'Awara Style

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Simmer the chicken and pork in water until nearly tender, about 30 minutes.
Stir in the ground peanuts to thicken and enrich the broth.
Add the cabbage and simmer 10-12 more minutes.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken, pork, and onion in 3 cups water for 30-35 minutes until nearly tender.

Blend the ground peanuts with a little of the hot broth into a smooth paste, then stir back into the pot along with the garlic - this stands in for awara, the rich palm fruit pulp traditionally used to give the broth its distinctive earthy, orange-red color and depth.
Add the cabbage and watercress, and simmer 12-15 more minutes until tender.
Bouillon d'awara is French Guiana's most treasured dish, traditionally prepared as a communal effort for Easter, often simmered for a full day with a dozen different meats and vegetables.

name: French Guianese Poulet Boucane
steps: Marinate the chicken with the garlic, lime juice, and paprika for at least 20 minutes.
Grill or roast at 425F for 30-35 minutes until deeply browned and smoky.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Marinate the chicken with the garlic, lime juice, paprika, oil, and cumin for at least 30 minutes, or overnight.
Grill over medium-high heat, or roast at 425F (220C), turning occasionally, 32-38 minutes until deeply browned, smoky, and cooked through.
Serve with rice - smoked and grilled chicken, echoing the boucané (smoking) tradition, is a beloved French Guianese preparation.

name: French Guianese Pork and Cassava Stew

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour

steps: Brown the pork with the onion.
Add the cassava, tomatoes, and a splash of water, and simmer, covered, 1-1.25 hours until the pork is tender.
Serve hot.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour

steps_detailed: Brown the pork with the onion, garlic, and jalapeno over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the cassava, diced tomatoes, and 3/4 cup water.
Cover and simmer over low heat 1-1.5 hours until the pork is completely tender and the cassava has softened into the stew.
A hearty, everyday French Guianese stew built on the region's staple root crop.

name: French Guianese Ragout de Cabri Style
steps: Brown the lamb with the onion and garlic.
Add the tomatoes and a splash of water, and simmer, covered, 1-1.25 hours until tender.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Brown the lamb (standing in for goat, traditionally used) with the onion, garlic, and paprika over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes and beef broth.
Cover and simmer over low heat 1.25-1.5 hours until completely tender and the sauce has thickened.
Serve with rice - ragout de cabri is a treasured French Guianese and French Caribbean dish, especially prominent at festive occasions.

name: French Guianese Acras de Morue
steps: Poach the fish until cooked through, then flake finely.
Mix with the flour and egg into a thick batter.
Fry spoonfuls until golden, about 3-4 minutes.
steps_detailed: Poach the fish until just cooked through, 8-10 minutes, then flake it very finely, checking for bones.
Mix the flaked fish with the flour, egg, garlic, and jalapeno into a thick, cohesive batter.
Fry spoonfuls in the oil over medium heat, 3-5 minutes, turning, until deeply golden and cooked through.
Acras, salt cod fritters, are a beloved snack shared across France's Caribbean and South American territories, especially popular at gatherings.

name: French Guianese Achards
steps: Toss the cabbage and carrot with the vinegar and jalapeno.
Let sit at least 20 minutes to pickle lightly before serving.
steps_detailed: Toss the shredded cabbage and carrot with the vinegar, jalapeno, garlic, and oil.
Season with salt and let sit at least 25-30 minutes so the vegetables lightly pickle.
Achards, a spicy pickled vegetable condiment, is a common French Guianese and French Caribbean accompaniment.

name: French Guianese Plantain Chips
steps: Slice the plantain as thin as possible.
Fry in the oil until golden and crisp, in batches, about 3-4 minutes per batch.
steps_detailed: Slice the plantain as thin and evenly as possible.
Fry in batches in the hot oil, 3-5 minutes per batch, until deeply golden and crisp, draining well between batches.
Finish with a light squeeze of lime - plantain chips are a common, well-loved French Guianese snack.

name: French Guianese Spiced Roasted Peanuts

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Toss the peanuts with the oil and paprika.
Warm briefly in a dry skillet, 2-3 minutes, and serve.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Toss the peanuts with the oil, paprika, and jalapeno if using.
Warm in a dry skillet over low heat, 2-4 minutes, tossing frequently, until fragrant.
A simple, spiced snack reflecting the peanut's importance across French Guianese cooking, especially in bouillon d'awara.

name: French Guianese Coconut Sweet (Doconon-Style)
steps: Simmer the shredded coconut with the sugar and coconut milk for 8-10 minutes until thick and sticky.
Press into a small dish and chill before cutting into squares.
steps_detailed: Simmer the shredded coconut with the sugar, coconut milk, and cinnamon over medium-low heat, stirring frequently, 10-12 minutes until thick, sticky, and fragrant.
Press firmly into a small lined dish and chill at least 1 hour until firm.
Cut into squares - a simple, sweet coconut treat found across French Guiana's Creole dessert tradition.

name: French Guianese Tourment d'Amour Style Tart
steps: Make a simple pastry crust from the flour and butter, and press into a small tart tin.
Mix the coconut with the sugar and a splash of milk, and spread over the crust.
Bake at 350F for 20-25 minutes until golden.
steps_detailed: Make a simple pastry from the flour, butter, a pinch of salt, and cold water. Press into a small tart tin and chill 15 minutes.
Mix the coconut with the sugar, egg, and milk into a thick, spreadable filling.
Spread over the chilled crust and bake at 350F (175C) for 22-28 minutes until golden and set.
This nods to tourment d'amour, a beloved coconut tart shared across France's Caribbean and South American territories, traditionally from the Iles des Saintes.

name: French Guianese Coco Flan
steps: Melt the sugar in a small dish until amber, then swirl to coat the bottom.
Whisk the eggs and coconut milk together and pour over the caramel.
Bake in a water bath at 325F for 35-40 minutes until just set.
steps_detailed: Melt the sugar in a small dish over low heat until deep amber, then carefully swirl to coat the bottom and sides.
Whisk the eggs, coconut milk, and vanilla together until smooth, then pour over the caramel.
Bake in a water bath at 325F (165C) for 35-45 minutes until just set with a gentle wobble.
Chill before unmolding onto a plate, letting the caramel run down the sides - a coconut-forward take on flan, reflecting French Guiana's blend of French and Creole dessert traditions.

name: French Guianese Sauce Chien
steps: Combine the onion, garlic, lime juice, and jalapeno.
Season with salt and let sit 10 minutes before serving alongside grilled fish or meat.
steps_detailed: Combine the onion, garlic, lime juice, jalapeno, cilantro, and oil.
Season with salt and let sit at least 10-15 minutes so the flavors meld.
Sauce chien, a bright, punchy condiment, is essential alongside grilled fish and meats across French Guiana and the French Caribbean.

name: French Guianese Peanut Sauce

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Simmer the ground peanuts with the tomatoes and garlic for 8-10 minutes until thickened.
Serve alongside grilled meats or rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Simmer the ground peanuts with the tomatoes, garlic, and jalapeno over medium-low heat, 10-12 minutes, until thickened into a rich, nutty sauce.
This peanut-forward sauce echoes the flavor base of bouillon d'awara, and pairs well with grilled meats or rice.
